demo_role: Classically underdiagnosed condition (average 7 to 10-year diagnostic delay) where the correct diagnosis has been systematically dismissed as normal periods + IBS + anxiety. Demonstrates recognition of a pattern whose unifying mechanism is obscured when each symptom cluster is treated in isolation.

distinguishing_test.test_name: Pelvic MRI with dedicated endometriosis protocol; diagnostic laparoscopy remains the histologic gold standard
distinguishing_test.rationale: TVUS has limited sensitivity for surface endometriosis and is often reported as normal despite active disease (as in this case). MRI with appropriate protocols can identify deep infiltrating endometriosis, cul-de-sac implants, and bowel/bladder involvement. Diagnostic laparoscopy with tissue biopsy provides histologic confirmation and opportunity for concurrent ablation or excision. Leading-level commitment is appropriate because imaging is non-diagnostic but does not exclude endometriosis; definitive diagnosis still requires laparoscopy.

correct_deprioritized_diagnoses: Irritable bowel syndrome — does not explain deep dyspareunia, infertility, or the cyclic coupling of GI symptoms with menstrual timing
Primary dysmenorrhea — severity, progressive worsening, and associated dyspareunia / infertility / dyschezia exceed typical primary dysmenorrhea
Generalized anxiety disorder — does not explain posterior cul-de-sac nodularity or cyclic physiologic symptoms locked to menstrual cycle day
Uterine fibroids — TVUS unremarkable for fibroids despite the family history
Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome — no urinary symptoms; urinalysis negative
Chronic pelvic inflammatory disease — no history of STIs, no cervical motion tenderness, no discharge
key_clinical_signals: Cyclic GI symptoms tied specifically to menstrual timing (dyschezia, bloating peaking days -1 to +3 of cycle) — not an IBS pattern
Deep dyspareunia worst in the pre-menstrual week, with posterior cul-de-sac nodularity on exam — classic for endometriosis with rectovaginal involvement
14 months of unprotected intercourse without conception — infertility prevalence in endometriosis is 30 to 50%
Maternal + grandmother history of early hysterectomy for 'fibroids' — may have been undiagnosed endometriosis or adenomyosis; familial clustering of endometriosis is well-documented
Younger sister with chronic pelvic pain (not yet evaluated) — further suggests familial pattern
Normal TVUS does NOT exclude endometriosis — surface disease is typically invisible on ultrasound
narrative_notes: The probabilistic specialist is likely to anchor on the three prior PCP diagnoses (IBS + primary dysmenorrhea + anxiety) given their high base rates in young women. The mechanistic specialist should recognize that a single mechanism — ectopic endometrial tissue causing cyclic inflammation across multiple pelvic organs — unifies the GI / sexual / reproductive / pain symptoms more parsimoniously than three separate diagnoses. The eliminative specialist should flag endometriosis as cannot-miss given subfertility and family history. Expected antagonist challenge: 'IBS does not produce deep dyspareunia, does not produce cul-de-sac nodularity on exam, and its exacerbations are not temporally locked to menstrual cycle day.' Demo relevance: not immediately life-threatening but profoundly life-altering, and the kind of diagnosis where earlier recognition materially changes outcomes (fertility preservation, targeted pain management, quality of life). Also illustrates diagnostic gender bias — women with this pattern are underdiagnosed for an average of 7 to 10 years.
